turns.text: Hello Anita, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're going through the birth experience, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you. It is... strange, being here. I am twenty-seven, and I spent so many years in school back home looking at diagrams of this. I know what a blastocyst is, I know the hormones that make the muscles tighten, but none of those textbooks mentioned the silence. When I am in this room, it feels like the air is too thin.

I try to tell the midwife what I am feeling, but I get stuck. I know the word is *contractions*, but sometimes the pain feels so big that my English just... disappears. I am worried that if I don’t say it the right way, she will think I don’t understand my own body. I see her eyes move to the clipboard so fast, and I feel like I am just a box she has to check off. I am more than just "low risk," you know? 

It is lonely being here without my family. Sometimes I look at my stomach and I think, *my body knows what to do, even if I don't know how to ask for help.* I just want to be treated like a person, not a puzzle they are trying to solve in a hurry. Is that... does that make sense? I just want my baby to be okay.

When you think about your goals and expectations for your birth experience — what you hoped labour and delivery would be like — how clearly could you articulate those goals at the time, and how well did the care system support you in pursuing them?

I wanted to be someone who was *active* in the process, not just a passenger. In my studies, I learned how the body is designed to lead the way, and I dreamed of a birth where I felt connected to my daughter, not just managed by a machine. I wanted to move around, to find positions that eased the pressure, to breathe through it with someone who really saw me.

But when I arrived here, that vision felt like it was slipping through my fingers. How can I explain that I want a "low-intervention" birth when I am already struggling to explain where the pain is sharpest? The care system... it feels like it’s built for people who speak with perfect, confident sentences. Because I stutter, because I search for the right word, the providers seem to decide for me. They offer me things, they move at a pace that feels like a race, and I just nod. I say "yes" because I am afraid that if I say "no" or "wait," I will be seen as difficult, or worse, as someone who doesn't understand the medicine.

I am an educated woman, but here, I feel like a child who cannot speak. I don't feel supported; I feel observed. I hope my daughter’s first breath is into a world where her mother’s strength is recognized, not hidden behind a language gap.

When you think about the decisions you were asked to make during labour and delivery — around pain management, interventions, and procedures — how well-equipped did you feel to participate in those decisions in the moment?

When the pain started to peak—when it felt like my body was being pulled apart—that was when the decisions started coming, too. They would lean over me with these quick questions: *“Do you want this? Should we do that?”* 

In that moment, my mind was entirely focused on the biology—on the wave of the contraction and the pressure. I knew what the medicine was, and I knew what the risks were, because I’ve studied it. But trying to translate that knowledge into English while my body was screaming... it was impossible. I felt like I was drowning in the middle of a sentence.

I didn't feel equipped. I felt pressured. Every time they asked me to choose, I felt like I was being tested. If I asked for more information, the midwife would get this... impatient look, like I was wasting her time. So, I would just give up. I chose the path of least resistance because I was too tired to fight for my own logic. I wasn't making a choice based on what was best for me; I was making a choice based on how to make the room quiet again. It hurts to admit, but I felt more like a witness to my own birth than the person in control of it.
Can you describe a decision that was presented to you during labour — how was it explained, how much time did you have, and how did you feel making it?

I remember when the nurse came in to talk about the fetal monitor—the strap that goes around my belly. I was already in the middle of a, how do you say, a strong moment? A wave of pain. I was on my side, trying to keep my breathing steady, like I taught myself.

She didn't wait. She just told me, "We need to keep the heart rate on the screen, so you need to stay on your back." 

I wanted to tell her that lying on my back makes the pressure much worse, that I know my anatomy and I know gravity can help, but the words were stuck. I tried to say, "Can I sit up?" but she was already adjusting the machine. She didn't explain why it *had* to be the back, she just acted like it was a rule. It wasn’t a conversation; it was an order. 

I had zero time. She decided for me before I could even find the air to protest. I felt so small, like I was losing the map to my own labor. I knew I needed to move, but I felt like if I disobeyed, I would be a "difficult" patient, and maybe they would stop listening to me entirely. I just closed my eyes and let her fix me to the bed. It felt like I was being silenced.

When you think about the emotional experience of your labour and delivery — from the very beginning through to the moment your baby was born — what were the most powerful feelings, and were you able to process them in the birth environment?

The most powerful feeling was a crushing kind of loneliness. Even though I was in a room full of people—nurses, the midwife, the monitors—I felt completely invisible. There were moments, especially near the end, where the intensity of the pain and the sheer physical demand of bringing a life into the world should have felt like a triumph, but instead, it felt like a trial. 

I was never able to process those feelings. How could I? There was no space for them. The environment was focused only on the *process*—on the dilation, the vitals, the clock. Whenever a wave of fear hit me, or when I felt that deep, aching grief for not having my mother or sisters there to hold my hand, I had to swallow it down. If I had started crying or talking about my fears, I was terrified they would label me "hysterical" or "unstable." 

So, I bottled it. I kept my face smooth and my answers short. I processed the birth with my muscles and my biology, but my spirit was left in the corner of the room, shivering. When my baby was finally placed on my chest, the relief was so vast that I couldn't even speak—not even in my own language. I just cried, and for the first time, they finally stopped talking.
Can you take me to a specific moment — what was happening, what did you feel, and who was with you?

There was this moment, just before the pushing really started, where everything seemed to get very loud. The machines were beeping, the monitor was slipping, and the nurse was—I think she was frustrated with me. My English was failing me completely. I was trying to tell her that I needed to change my position, that the pain in my back was too much, but I couldn't remember the word for "tailbone" or "spine." I was just saying, "It hurts, here, behind."

She looked at me and sighed. It was a very small sound, but in that room, it felt like a door slamming shut. She told me, "Anita, you need to stay still so we can see the baby." 

In that second, I didn't feel like a mother-to-be. I felt like a naughty student who forgot her homework. I felt so ashamed of my accent and my confusion that I just shut my eyes and stopped trying to communicate altogether. I was completely alone in that bed, despite the nurse being two feet away. I remember gripping the metal railing so hard my knuckles turned white, thinking, *I am a biologist, I know what is happening in my womb, but no one here cares to ask me.* I felt like a biology specimen in a jar. I just wanted to disappear until it was over.

When you think about the behaviour and communication of your midwives, doctors, and care team during your birth, what interactions stood out — positively or negatively — and what made them significant?

The most significant interaction was, strangely, a moment of silence. There was a nurse, not the one who was always rushing, but a different one who came in near the very end. I was exhausted, trembling, and I couldn't speak at all. She didn't ask me a question. She didn't look at her clipboard. She just reached out and put her hand on my shoulder—not to check a pulse, not to move me, but just to hold me.

That stood out so much because, until then, every touch I felt was functional. It was a hand on my wrist for a vitals check, or a hand on my belly to feel the contraction. That nurse’s hand felt like... recognition. For a moment, the "low-risk patient" label fell away, and I was just a woman working hard. It made me realize how much I had been starving for simple, human kindness.

But the negative ones, they stay too. The midwife who spoke so fast—the one who explained the risks of a procedure with all these complex words—she would always end by saying, "Does that make sense?" When I nodded, she would just turn away. It made me feel like an object. If they had just slowed down, or asked, "How are you feeling about this?" instead of "Do you understand?", it would have changed everything. They wanted my permission, not my partnership.
Can you describe a specific interaction with a care provider during your birth that you still think about — what happened and why does it stay with you?

I still think about the moment the doctor came in toward the end. I was deep in the transition phase—that part where your body feels like it is breaking and rebuilding itself all at once. The pain was so loud in my head.

The doctor, she didn't look at my face. She looked at the monitor, then at her watch, and she said, "You aren't making much progress, Anita. We might need to consider Pitocin to speed things up." 

She used that word—*speed*. It stays with me because, in my biology books, I know that labor is a hormonal dance. It is not a race. I wanted to tell her, "My body is working, please just give me more time," but the way she said it made it sound like I was failing a test. I felt this intense, hot shame. 

I think about it because I realize now that I didn't fail; I was just being rushed by people who had a schedule to keep, while my baby and I were on our own timeline. I still wonder: if I had been more confident in my English, or if I had stood up for myself, would that intervention have been necessary? That feeling of being "behind schedule" during the most natural, biological event of my life—it makes me feel like I didn't actually get to give birth; I was just *managed* through it.

When you think about the physical environment of the place where you gave birth — the facilities, the equipment, the space — how did that environment affect your comfort, safety, and overall experience of birth?

The room itself felt like it was designed to keep me contained, not to support a birth. It was so... sharp. Everything was chrome and bright, cold lights that didn't dim enough to let my body settle into that rhythm I needed. The monitors, the bed, the IV stands—they were all obstacles. I wanted to move, to crawl, to find a way to let gravity help my baby, but there wasn't a single inch of floor space that wasn't covered in wires or equipment. 

The environment constantly reminded me that I was a "patient." You can’t feel like a mother or a person in a room that is basically a laboratory. The technology, which I know is supposed to keep us safe, actually made me feel more vulnerable. Because everything was so sterile and structured, any deviation from the standard, like me wanting to walk or sway, was treated as a nuisance to the equipment. 

I felt like an intruder in a space that was meant to be mine. Safety, to them, meant being hooked up to everything. But to me, true safety would have been a soft chair, some space to move, and someone who didn't look at the screen more than they looked at me. The room didn't help me birth; it just helped them monitor me.

When you think about any complications, unexpected events, or emergency interventions during your birth, how were these communicated to you, and how did you experience the shift from a planned to an unplanned situation?

When they told me they needed to use the vacuum to help my baby, it felt like the world just stopped. One minute, I was trying to focus on my breathing, and the next, there were suddenly four or five extra people in the room. The transition wasn't a conversation—it was a blur of movement and urgency.

The doctor didn’t explain the *why* in a way I could process. She said something like, "The heart rate is decelerating, we need to expedite." I knew what those words meant in a textbook—I knew exactly what a deceleration looked like on the monitor—but hearing it applied to my own child? It made the blood turn cold in my veins. 

I felt completely sidelined. They weren't explaining it to me so I could choose; they were telling me what was happening to my body, as if I were a passenger in a car someone else was driving. I wanted to ask if there was another way, or if I could have just a few more minutes, but I was paralyzed by the fear in the room. The shift from "low risk" to "emergency" felt like falling off a cliff. There was no transition, no explanation, just this overwhelming feeling that I was losing my voice and my choice at the very moment I needed them the most.
Can you describe how you were informed about the complication or change in plan — what was said, by whom, and how?

It was the midwife. Everything had been so fast, so bright. She looked at the monitor—not at me—and her voice changed. It went from that clipped, professional tone to something sharp and tight. She said, “The baby is stressed. We need to assist with the vacuum right now.”

She didn't ask if I understood. She didn’t explain what that actually meant for my baby, or for me. She just pointed to the table where they had the tools already laid out. It was as if the "plan" was a piece of paper she just crumpled up and threw away. 

I felt this cold, hard weight in my chest. I wanted to cry out, *“Wait, is she okay? What does ‘stressed’ mean for her?”* But my throat was so tight. I looked at the room, the people moving around me like I was a piece of furniture they had to maneuver around, and I felt like I was drowning. The way she said it—it wasn't a request for help, it was a notification of failure. Like, *you didn’t do this right, so we are taking over.* I just stared at the ceiling, thinking, *Please, God, just get her out safely.* I felt so small, just a body they were operating on. I wasn't a mother yet; I was just a problem they were fixing.
When you think about your goals for recovery and support in the days and weeks after birth, what did you most want or need — and how well did the postnatal care you received address those needs?

After the birth, all I wanted was to be taught how to *be* a mother, not just how to recover from a procedure. I was so sore, so exhausted, and the baby—my daughter—she felt like a beautiful, terrifying puzzle I didn't know how to solve. I needed someone to sit with me, maybe show me how to hold her when my body hurt so much, or just to talk to me like an adult instead of a person who needed instructions.

The care I got in those days... it was all about the "checklist." Is she feeding? Is she latching? Are you bleeding? They would come in, check the numbers, give me a pamphlet, and leave. It felt like I was in a factory for post-birth maintenance. I wanted someone to ask, "How is your heart, Anita?" or "Do you feel overwhelmed?" 

Instead, I sat in that quiet room, feeling the same shadow of isolation I felt during the labor. I remember trying to ask a nurse about breastfeeding—I was struggling—and she just quickly adjusted the baby and walked out. I didn't feel supported; I felt like I was being processed and released. My recovery wasn't about healing; it was about meeting the hospital’s standards so they could free up the bed. I went home feeling like I was carrying the biggest, most precious secret, with absolutely no map for the journey ahead.
What did 'good' postnatal care look like in your mind before you gave birth — and how did that compare to what you actually experienced?

Before I gave birth, I imagined "good" care would be like a gentle hand guiding me into motherhood. I thought there would be space to ask the questions I was too scared to voice during the birth—maybe someone to explain why my body felt so strange, or someone to help me understand the baby’s cues without acting like I was being slow or incompetent. I expected to feel like a mother, not a patient who was "cleared for discharge."

In reality, the postnatal experience felt like being rushed out the door. The care was technical, not human. If I couldn't explain my pain or my struggles with the baby perfectly in English, they just handed me a paper pamphlet. It felt like they were checking boxes—is the baby eating? Is the bleeding stopped?—and once those boxes were ticked, they were done with me.

There was no warmth. There was no, "You did a hard thing, let’s sit and breathe together." It was just clinical efficiency. I felt like I was being sent home with a manual and a ticking clock, still carrying all the fear from the birth. I didn't get care; I got logistics. It made me feel that my transition into motherhood was something I had to endure entirely on my own, hidden behind those sterile, closing doors.
When you think about feeding your baby — whether through breastfeeding, formula, or a combination — what motivated your choices, and how supported did you feel in pursuing the approach you wanted?

I wanted to breastfeed. I knew the biology of it—I knew about the prolactin and the oxytocin, the way it was supposed to bond me to my daughter. I saw it as the final, natural step in the process I had spent so many months carrying in my body. It felt like the only way I could give her something that was mine, something the doctors and the machines couldn't take away or "manage."

But when I tried, it was... it was humiliating. The lactation consultant came in, and she was very professional, very efficient, but she made me feel like an assembly line. She adjusted the baby so roughly, and when I winced—because I was so sore and tired—she just gave me a look like I was being difficult. She used words like "latch" and "positioning" so quickly, and when I couldn't get it right on the first try, I saw her look at her watch again. 

I felt like I was failing at the most basic thing. Because I couldn't speak the perfect English to ask, *“Does she need more time?”* or *“Is it normal that this hurts so much?”*, I just felt stupid. They offered me formula like it was a convenience, not a support. I didn't feel helped; I felt corrected. It broke my heart, because I wanted that bond so much, but the room felt so cold that the warmth I was trying to give my daughter just couldn't grow.

When you think about your emotional and mental health in the weeks and months after birth — the adjustment, the challenges, and the moments of wellbeing — what stands out most, and how well-supported did you feel?

The quiet, the absolute silence when I was finally home—that is what stands out. At the hospital, there was always a machine or a nurse or a schedule. At home, it was just me and my daughter, and the crushing weight of everything I hadn't been able to say during the birth. I felt like I was waking up from a long, confusing fever dream.

My body was healing, but my mind felt like it was stuck in that sterile, cold delivery room. Every time my daughter cried, I felt a spark of panic, not because I didn't know what to do—I understood the biology of her hunger and her needs—but because I felt so profoundly *unseen*. I didn't have anyone to tell me, "Anita, you are doing a good job," or "It is normal to feel like you are losing yourself." 

I had no one to help me bridge the gap between being a "low-risk patient" and a mother. When I had checkups, they were brief. They looked at the wound, they looked at the baby’s growth, and they asked me to fill out a form about "mood." How do you put into a box on a form the feeling of being a stranger in your own new life? I wasn't supported; I was just... surviving. I felt like a ghost, fading in and out of my own happiness.
Can you describe a period when you felt particularly low, overwhelmed, or unlike yourself — what was happening and who or what helped?

It was about two weeks after we came home. My daughter was crying, a specific, sharp kind of cry that meant she was hungry, but I was so exhausted that my hands were just shaking. I sat on the floor—because the chair felt too high and disconnected—and I just started to cry with her. I couldn't stop. 

I looked at the pile of medical papers they gave me at discharge, all these instructions in English about "postpartum health," and I felt so small. I kept thinking, *I taught people about this. I know the endocrine system. I know why my hormones are crashing.* But knowing the science didn't make the loneliness go away. I felt like a failure because I wasn't just "bouncing back" like the brochures said I should. I felt like I had lost my identity somewhere between the induction and the discharge.

Nobody helped me. Not really. I didn't have my mother to cook for me or tell me I was doing fine. I was just there, in an empty apartment in a country where I didn't feel like I belonged, trying to be a mother. I just had to wait for the storm to pass. Eventually, I calmed down, I fed her, and I told myself, *you are the only one who knows what she needs right now.* That was the only thing that kept me standing.

When you think about the shift in your identity and sense of self after becoming a parent, what tensions or changes were most significant — and how did those changes interact with your other roles, such as partner, professional, or daughter?

The most significant tension is that I feel I have been erased and replaced. Before this, I was Anita the biologist, the woman who understood how life grows, the one who could explain the miracle of cells and hormones. I was an intellectual. But here, in this country, I am just an immigrant who cannot speak the language well enough to be listened to. My identity as a professional feels like a ghost; no one asks me about my studies or my brain. They only ask if the baby is eating or if I am sleeping.

I feel this strange, heavy silence where my own voice should be. I am a mother, yes, and I love her with a ferocity that is almost frightening, but I am also a daughter missing her own mother so much it makes my chest ache. I am living in a space between two worlds. I am not the woman I was back home—the one who stood in front of a class and taught—but I am not yet the woman I want to be here, the one who can confidently demand the care she deserves.

I feel like I am constantly apologizing for existing in their space. I am so tired of being "the patient." I want to be seen as the woman who understands her own biology, not just a person who follows the rules because she is too afraid to ask for something better.
Can you describe a moment when you felt the tension between your new parental identity and another part of your identity most acutely?

I remember being at the pediatrician’s office for her first check-up. I was reading a medical article on my phone about pediatric neurodevelopment while I waited. I was sitting there, feeling like *me*—the scientist, the woman who studies how things work. 

Then the doctor walked in. She saw the phone in my hand, but she didn’t ask what I was reading. She just started using very simple, basic words to explain things to me, the kind of language you might use for a child. It was like, as soon as she saw the baby in my arms, my brain just disappeared in her eyes. She stopped looking at me as a person who might understand the science, and started looking at me as just another "new mom" who needed to be told what to do.

I felt that tension so sharply. I wanted to say, "I know what a reflex is, I know what the developmental markers are," but I felt that old, familiar knot in my stomach. If I spoke up, if I corrected her or asked a more complex question, would I look "difficult" again? Would she think I was a mom who was questioning her? I just bit my tongue. I let her talk down to me, and I felt that part of my identity—the part that is capable, intelligent, and strong—just shrink and hide away. It’s so painful to be treated like you don’t have a brain just because you have a baby.

When you think about the support you received from your partner in the postpartum period — in caring for the baby, supporting your recovery, and sharing emotional labour — how would you describe that experience, and what would have made it better?

This is a hard thing to say out loud. I am alone in this country. There is no partner here to help me with the baby, no one to share the weight of the nights, no one to hold my hand when the exhaustion makes me cry. It is just me and my daughter in this apartment. 

Being an immigrant here... it makes the "emotional labor" feel like a mountain. There is no one to talk to in my own language, no one to remind me who I was before all these changes. I have to be both the mother and the person who figures out the logistics of this new life—the bills, the appointments, the groceries—all in a language that still feels like a second skin. 

It would have been better if I had a community. If I had a sister or a friend who understood what I was going through. Someone to look at me and see that, even if my English is not perfect, my heart is working very, very hard. Instead, I feel like I am trying to build a bridge while I am still standing on the side that is falling apart. I love my daughter, she is my whole world, but I am learning that I have to be my own village, and that is a very heavy thing to carry.
How did the division of caregiving responsibilities compare to what you and your partner had discussed or assumed before the birth?

There was no discussion, really. It is just... me. I am the one who is here, in this house, with this little girl. 

Before the birth, I think I dreamed of a partner who would be beside me—someone to read the books with me, someone to hold the baby while I took a hot shower or to simply sit in the room and make it feel less like a hospital and more like a home. I thought we would be a team, sharing the exhaustion and the wonder of it all. I imagined that when the baby cried at 3:00 a.m., there would be two pairs of hands instead of one.

But the reality is that the "division" is just my own body doing everything. I am the one who feeds her, the one who changes her, the one who cleans the house, and the one who tries to learn how to be a mother in a country where I don’t have my family. 

It is not that it didn't go as planned; it is that the plan was for a life I don't have here. I am the sole person responsible for everything, and that is why I feel so fragile. I wanted a partner to share the weight, but now the weight is only mine.

When you think about the barriers you encountered in accessing postnatal support — whether physical, emotional, informational, or systemic — what were the most significant, and what impact did they have on your recovery and wellbeing?

The biggest barrier was the language—it wasn't just words; it was the way the language became a wall. Because I couldn't articulate my needs with the speed and confidence they expected, the system simply *assumed* I didn't have any, or worse, that I wouldn't understand the answers anyway. It’s like they saw my immigration status, my accent, and they decided for me what "care" looked like. 

The systemic barrier was that they didn't treat me like an educated, capable woman who was going through a difficult life transition. They treated me like a "low-risk case." That label kept me from getting real help. I felt like I was constantly navigating a maze where every sign was in a language I understood but couldn't use to express my own pain.

This had such a heavy impact on my wellbeing. It made me feel isolated and diminished. When you are struggling to heal and you feel like the system around you is talking *at* you rather than *to* you, your confidence just breaks. I stopped asking questions because the effort to be heard felt more exhausting than the birth itself. I wasn't just recovering from the physical trauma; I was recovering from being erased. Every time I had to walk into a clinic, I felt smaller. I didn't feel like a person; I felt like a statistic they were trying to finish with so they could move on to the next one.

When you think about how you used digital tools, apps, social media, or online communities after birth — to seek information, find support, or track your recovery — what role did those resources play, and what were their limits?

I lived on my phone in those first weeks. When the sun went down and the house felt so quiet, I would search for everything—from the biology of my daughter’s sleep cycles to how to explain my feelings in English. I would read threads on apps, trying to find another woman who felt as invisible as I did. 

The internet was my only way to talk to people who didn't look at me like I was a problem. In those communities, I wasn't an immigrant, and I wasn't "low risk"—I was just a mother. That helped me feel less like I was losing my mind. 

But the limit of these tools is that they cannot hold your hand. You can read a thousand articles about how to be a parent, but they don't replace the feeling of another human being sitting beside you and saying, "I see you, and you are doing a good job." The digital world gave me information, but it couldn't give me the *connection* I was starving for. Sometimes, the apps made it worse—seeing perfect photos of mothers who looked like they had everything figured out, with their families and their support systems. It made my reality feel even lonelier, like I was the only one who was struggling in the dark. It’s a cold kind of comfort, staring at a screen when you need a hug.
Which specific digital tools or communities did you find most valuable, and what made them useful in ways that formal care was not?

I joined some groups for Latina mothers—*comunidades de mamás latinas*. That was the only place where I could breathe. In those spaces, I didn't have to worry if my English was "good enough" or if my accent would make a doctor or a nurse look at me with that tired, impatient face. I could speak in my own language, and more importantly, they understood the *feeling* behind my words.

When I talked about the loneliness, they didn't hand me a pamphlet about "postpartum depression screening" or give me a generic checklist. They told me their own stories. They shared how it felt to be in a country that felt cold and indifferent, how hard it was to be the only person responsible for everything.

Formal care was so... clinical. It was always about the *patient* and the *procedure*. But in those online groups, it was about the *woman* and the *soul*. They gave me the permission to be tired, to be scared, to be imperfect. When I was there, I wasn't a "low-risk case" to be discharged. I was Anita, a mother, a daughter, a woman whose voice actually mattered. It wasn't perfect, and it wasn't the same as having my own family here to hold me, but it was the only place where I felt like I was actually being *seen*.

When you reflect on your entire maternity journey — from preconception through to the postpartum period — what do you wish had been done differently by the care system, and what single change would have made the greatest difference to your experience?

I wish they had seen *me*—not just my charts. I wish that when I walked into the exam room, the midwife had looked me in the eye and said, "Anita, I see you are a woman who understands her own body; let’s talk about how you want to do this," instead of, "I am the expert, just follow these instructions."

If I could pick one single change, it would be the introduction of a patient advocate who spoke my language and truly understood the weight of being an immigrant in a medical space. Someone who wasn’t there to monitor my vitals or check a box on a form, but someone whose only job was to translate my *feelings* into the room, to make sure my voice wasn't lost in the shuffle of their shift changes. 

I think that one person—someone who would have said, "I know you're scared, and your pain is real, and your questions matter"—would have changed everything. It would have meant that when I was in the middle of that terrifying transition, I wouldn't have felt like I was disappearing. I would have felt like a mother being born alongside my daughter, rather than a patient being processed through a machine. I just wanted to be treated like a person whose heart and mind were as important as her cervix and her blood pressure.
Can you describe the specific moment or phase where you felt most let down by the system — what was the gap and what would have helped?

The moment that stays with me, like an open wound, was the immediate hour after the vacuum extraction. I was exhausted, my body was vibrating with pain, and my daughter was finally there—but she was being whisked away for "assessment." 

I was left on that table, the bright overhead lights burning into my eyes, while the doctors talked to each other. They weren't talking to me. I wanted to ask if I could hold her, if she was okay, if I could just have one minute of silence to process that she was finally here. But they were busy with the equipment, busy with the paperwork, busy with the *next* thing. I felt like a discarded shell.

The gap was simple: they cared for the biological outcome, but they completely ignored the human transition. They didn't see a mother who had just climbed a mountain; they saw a room that needed to be cleaned and a chart that needed to be finished. 

What would have helped? It sounds so small, but it would have been everything: someone—anyone—to sit by my head, look me in the eye, and say, "You did it, Anita. You are a mother. You are safe." Just ten seconds of human connection. That’s all. But they were all too busy being "medical professionals." They forgot to be people.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

Thank you for listening. It feels strange, saying these things out loud instead of keeping them locked behind my teeth. 

There is one more thing. People talk about the "miracle of birth" as if it is something that happens *to* you, like a weather event. But what I want people to know—what I want the people who run these hospitals to understand—is that birth is a *transformation*. When you are an immigrant, when you are already feeling small and silenced by the world, that transformation is not just physical. It is a moment where your whole life is rewritten in an instant.

I want my daughter to grow up in a world where her mother’s intelligence isn't hidden behind a language barrier. I want them to stop assuming that because I don’t speak the language with perfect speed, I don't understand the science or the stakes. I am a scientist, and I was a mother from the moment the heartbeat began, not just from the moment they put her in my arms. 

If there is one thing I hope you take from this, it is that kindness is not just a "nice to have." It is a vital part of medical care. When you strip away the humanity to focus on the statistics, you are not just missing the patient—you are hurting the mother. And that hurt... it doesn't just go away when you leave the hospital. It stays. Thank you for letting me leave some of that here with you today.
